steps: Each kilogram of soil contains 600 mg of phosphorus, so a sample of \(x\) kilograms contains \(600x\) milligrams total.
The problem states the total is 1,200 mg, giving the equation \(600x = 1{,}200\).
Solving: \(x = 2\) kg, which is a sensible positive mass.

choices.explanation:
• This adds \(x\) (kg) to 600 (mg/kg), mixing incompatible quantities without multiplication.
  • It would give \(x = 600\), ignoring the per-kilogram rate entirely.
  • Eliminated because the rate relationship requires multiplication, not addition.

• This treats 1,200 as the rate and 600 as the total, reversing the given information.
  • It yields \(x = 0.5\) kg, but substituting back: \(600 \times 0.5 = 300\) mg, not 1,200 mg.
  • Eliminated because the rate and total are swapped.

• This requires \(x = -600\), a negative mass, which is physically impossible.
  • It also adds incompatible units (kg and mg) without a rate conversion.
  • Eliminated because no real soil sample has negative mass.

• Rate \(\times\) mass = total: \(600 \text{ mg/kg} \times x \text{ kg} = 1{,}200 \text{ mg}\).
  • This directly models the situation and solves to \(x = 2\) kg.
  • That matches choice D — D is correct.
